verses.text: The princes report that the people, priests, and Levites have intermarried with the pagan nations, so 'the holy seed' has mingled with the unclean. The sin reaches even the leaders. Compromise with the world spreads quietly until it threatens the very identity of God's people.

The princes report that the people, priests, and Levites have intermarried with the pagan nations, so 'the holy seed' has mingled with the unclean. The sin reaches even the leaders. Compromise with the world spreads quietly until it threatens the very identity of God's people.

Ezra tears his garments, pulls his hair, and sits down appalled, while those who tremble at God's word gather to him. The sin strikes him to the heart. A tender conscience feels the weight of evil that careless hearts shrug off.

Ezra tears his garments, pulls his hair, and sits down appalled, while those who tremble at God's word gather to him. The sin strikes him to the heart. A tender conscience feels the weight of evil that careless hearts shrug off.

At the evening sacrifice Ezra spreads his hands and confesses, 'our iniquities are increased over our head,' owning the long guilt of the nation. He prays as one of the sinners, not above them. True intercession bears the people's sin before God as its own.

At the evening sacrifice Ezra spreads his hands and confesses, 'our iniquities are increased over our head,' owning the long guilt of the nation. He prays as one of the sinners, not above them. True intercession bears the people's sin before God as its own.

At the evening sacrifice Ezra spreads his hands and confesses, 'our iniquities are increased over our head,' owning the long guilt of the nation. He prays as one of the sinners, not above them. True intercession bears the people's sin before God as its own.

Yet, Ezra acknowledges, God has shown grace for a moment, leaving a remnant and giving a reviving in their bondage and a wall of protection. Mercy shines even in confession. The penitent remembers God's kindness, which makes the sin against it the more grievous.

Yet, Ezra acknowledges, God has shown grace for a moment, leaving a remnant and giving a reviving in their bondage and a wall of protection. Mercy shines even in confession. The penitent remembers God's kindness, which makes the sin against it the more grievous.

'What shall we say after this?' Ezra cries, recalling God's clear command not to bind themselves to the pagan nations. The law leaves them no excuse. Sin is most painful when we see it was committed against light plainly given.

'What shall we say after this?' Ezra cries, recalling God's clear command not to bind themselves to the pagan nations. The law leaves them no excuse. Sin is most painful when we see it was committed against light plainly given.

'What shall we say after this?' Ezra cries, recalling God's clear command not to bind themselves to the pagan nations. The law leaves them no excuse. Sin is most painful when we see it was committed against light plainly given.

After all God's mercy, Ezra asks, should they break His commands again, when they cannot even stand before Him as it is? He casts the people wholly on God's righteousness. Genuine repentance pleads no merit but throws itself on mercy alone.
